title: Airlines — Jet Fuel Cost Relief from Iran Peace Deal & Hormuz Reopening
summary: Iran-US peace deal signed June 19 and the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz are collapsing the jet fuel premium that was crushing Q2 airline earnings. Q2 guidance was set on $4.30/gal jet fuel assumptions; with Brent back below $80, that assumption is now too pessimistic and creates an asymmetric estimate-beat setup into Q3.
catalyst: On June 19, 2026 the US and Iran signed a memorandum to end their four-month war and reopen the Strait of Hormuz toll-free for at least 60 days. Brent crude fell from above $112 to ~$77 within a week. Delta had guided Q2 EPS of $1.00-$1.50 on a $4.30/gal fuel assumption (vs. $2.62/gal in Q1) that contributed to a ~$2B fuel cost squeeze in Q2 alone — that headwind is now reversing in real time and Q3 guidance becomes the catalyst.
thesis: Fuel is roughly 18% of total airline opex. The market priced in a sustained war premium when these stocks compressed to single-digit P/Es and management cut capacity. With oil already retracing and the war over, the Q3 fuel assumption will reset materially lower, restoring the ~$2B/quarter cost line and rebuilding earnings power. Delta is the cleanest expression: best balance sheet in the group, the only major with its own refinery (which adds $300M+ of pretax benefit in a normalized crack-spread environment), and a 10.4x forward P/E that already discounts a recession.
counter_thesis: Peace deals in the Middle East rarely survive 60 days without challenge — a single tanker incident could re-spike oil and re-impose the fuel headwind. Demand is the second leg: airlines cut capacity in Q2 in response to high fuel, so even if fuel falls, traffic and yield may also have softened, and Bastian's comment that 2026 capacity will be 'flat on the year' implies a revenue ceiling. Bear case is that Q3 EPS still misses because: (1) bookings are softer than seat-mile cuts, (2) the Fed turned hawkish on June 17 and the macro backdrop is weakening, and (3) labor costs continue to escalate from new pilot contracts. Finally, the cleanest 'peace dividend' trade is also the most crowded — airlines popped on the June 14 announcement and giving back gains on any setback.

ticker_rationale.DAL: Best-in-class balance sheet (BBB rated, only investment-grade airline) and owns the Trainer, PA refinery — uniquely positioned to capture both lower jet fuel input cost and refinery margin on the reversal. Trades at 10.4x forward P/E with IOWN 73 (HOLD). Q1 fuel was $2.62/gal; Q2 guided to $4.30/gal; a normalized $3.00/gal Q3 would unwind ~$1.5B of the cost guide.
ticker_rationale.UAL: Largest international exposure of the US3 — recovers fastest as Middle East/Asia routes normalize and fuel surcharges unwind. 9.96x forward P/E, IOWN 67. Operating leverage is highest of the three because international yields are most fuel-sensitive.
ticker_rationale.LUV: Pure domestic, no fuel hedges as of latest disclosure, so 100% of fuel-cost decline flows through. 10.6x forward P/E. IOWN 68. Lower beta than UAL but cleanest read on US consumer demand if the macro backdrop cooperates.

trade_construction.stop_loss: DAL $72 (-14%), UAL $98 (-15%), LUV $42 (-12%) on a closing basis
trade_construction.position_size_pct: 0.75-1.0% per name (On Our Radar); add 0.5% to leader (DAL) post-Q2 earnings if fuel relief shows through in actual realized prices

confidence_note: Promotes to High Conviction when: (1) DAL Q2 actual fuel realized comes in materially below the $4.30 guide AND Q3 guide is set on ≤$3.20/gal, OR (2) Brent stays below $80 for 30+ consecutive days post-peace-deal signing. Current stop levels are approximate — refine after Q2 prints (mid-July).
risks: Iran peace deal collapses within the 60-day toll-free Hormuz window, re-spiking oil above $90 and reversing the fuel-relief thesis
Demand softens faster than fuel relief flows through — Fed hawkish turn on June 17 plus consumer fatigue compresses unit revenue even as fuel falls
Capacity discipline breaks: a competitor (most likely AAL or JBLU) restores summer capacity to chase share, pressuring industry yields
Pilot/labor cost escalation from new contracts (DAL pilots Q3 negotiation; LUV flight-attendant deal pending) absorbs the fuel windfall
Hedging dynamics: any airline with significant fuel hedges layered in at $90+ Brent would not realize the spot-price relief

invalidation.trigger: Brent crude rebounds above $90/bbl for 5 consecutive trading days on Iran/Hormuz reescalation
DAL Q2 actual fuel realized comes in at $4.30/gal or higher AND Q3 guidance is set above $3.50/gal
US3 unit revenue (RASM) declines by 5%+ year-over-year on Q3 print, signaling demand destruction is outpacing fuel relief

sources.title: Delta Air Lines Q1 2026 10-Q — fuel was 18% of operating expense, $2.62/gal average
sources.publisher: SEC EDGAR / Delta Air Lines IR

sources.title: Delta Q1 2026 earnings call — Q2 fuel guided $4.30/gal, ~$2B cost increase, capacity cut
Strait of Hormuz reopening: oil prices tumble, airlines climb
FOMC June 17, 2026 Statement — rates 3.5-3.75%, hawkish bias signals macro caution
